globalOpportunities.name: Google Solution Challenge
globalOpportunities.eligibility: University student teams (high school: prep & observer)
globalOpportunities.deadline: Applications typically open Aug–Sep; submission deadline Jan–Feb
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: Build an app addressing one or more of the UN's 17 Sustainable Development Goals using Google technology.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: Although the competition targets university GDSC chapters, its past winners (AI-powered waste sorting, accessible navigation apps) and open developer resources are excellent roadmaps for high schoolers. Study the winning projects to understand what impactful AI product development looks like, and use the same stack in your own projects.
tips: Team up with a college GDSC chapter as a collaborator
Pick one SDG and solve a hyper-local version of the problem
Use Gemini API for rapid NLP features without heavy ML infrastructure

globalOpportunities.name: Technovation Girls
globalOpportunities.nameCn: 全球女性科技创新挑战赛
globalOpportunities.deadline: Register Oct–Dec; project submission typically April
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: A global non-profit program encouraging girls aged 8–18 to build mobile apps or AI outputs that solve real community problems.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: If your AI Club has female members, this is the top recommendation. Teams write a business plan alongside their app, learning both technical and entrepreneurial skills. Past winners have tackled climate change, mental health, and food insecurity — the scope is intentionally broad. There is no enrollment fee and support resources are excellent.
tips: Focus on a problem the team personally experiences — judges value authenticity
The business plan section is taken as seriously as the app — prepare both
Reach out to Technovation ambassadors in your region for mentorship

globalOpportunities.name: The Earth Prize
globalOpportunities.deadline: Applications open Nov; deadline typically March
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: A global award for 13–19 year olds using innovative technology to solve environmental sustainability challenges. Prize: up to $100,000 implementation grant.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: The Earth Prize is one of the few competitions that explicitly targets high schoolers and offers a six-figure implementation grant rather than a trophy. The judging criteria reward scientific rigor alongside innovation — teams that use AI for remote sensing, satellite data analysis, or predictive modeling of environmental systems consistently perform well. No prior competition experience required.
tips: Pair AI/ML prediction with real environmental datasets (NASA Earthdata, NOAA) for maximum impact
The implementation plan matters — judges want to see how the prize money would be used
Partner with a local environmental organization to validate your problem framing

globalOpportunities.name: ITU AI for Good Youth Summit
globalOpportunities.nameCn: 国际电信联盟 AI for Good 青年峰会
globalOpportunities.eligibility: Youth delegates and student innovators worldwide
globalOpportunities.deadline: Annual event — Geneva (May–June); applications open 3–4 months prior
techStack: Any AI/ML stack relevant to your chosen SDG
globalOpportunities.shortDesc: The United Nations' premier platform for AI innovation with social impact. Youth Summit sessions and student project showcases run alongside the main conference.
globalOpportunities.whyHighSchool: Participating in an ITU AI for Good event — even as a virtual delegate — puts your work in front of international policymakers, UN agency representatives, and global AI researchers. Student projects showcased here have gone on to receive UN accelerator support. The exposure is unmatched for any other student competition.
tips: Submit a project proposal months in advance for the best chance at the student showcase
Frame your project using official UN SDG language — it helps with evaluation criteria
Virtual participation is often free — follow ITU AI for Good on social media for early announcements

clubProjects.name: Project A: Multilingual Minority Dialect Translator
clubProjects.nameCn: 多语言少数族裔方言翻译器
clubProjects.desc: An open-source tool that translates between minority dialect languages (Cantonese, Hokkien, Hmong, Armenian, and others commonly spoken in the LA/Irvine area) to reduce language barriers in educational settings. Built with fine-tuned multilingual models and community-contributed dialect data.
clubProjects.howToContribute: Add dialect data samples, improve translation quality for a specific language pair, or help with the front-end interface. No advanced ML experience needed — data collection and annotation are equally important.

clubProjects.name: Project B: LA/Irvine Air Quality Prediction Model
clubProjects.nameCn: 洛杉矶/尔湾空气质量预测大模型
clubProjects.desc: A time-series forecasting model trained on SCAQMD (South Coast Air Quality Management District) sensor data, predicting PM2.5, ozone, and NO₂ levels 24–72 hours ahead for neighborhoods across Los Angeles and Orange County. Deployed as a public API and interactive map.
clubProjects.howToContribute: Help add new sensor data sources, improve the forecasting model architecture, contribute to the web visualization, or write documentation. Community air quality monitors (PurpleAir) data integration is the current top priority.

featured.name: CrowdSource by Google
featured.desc: A public platform where anyone can contribute to making AI fairer and more inclusive — through image labeling, handwriting recognition, translation validation, and more. Each small contribution helps train AI models that work better for all languages, cultures, and communities.
featured.whyJoin: The perfect entry point for students with no coding experience who still want to contribute to AI development. You can start in minutes, and your contributions directly reduce bias in Google's production AI systems. It's also a conversation-starter in applications: 'I contributed to AI fairness at Google.'

featured.name: OpenStreetMap × AI Integration
featured.desc: Use computer vision to automatically identify buildings, roads, and infrastructure from satellite imagery, then contribute human-verified annotations for accessibility features (ramps, tactile paving, accessible entrances). HOT (Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team) coordinates disaster response mapping missions where your contributions directly support humanitarian aid operations.
featured.whyJoin: Directly relevant if you have any interest in geospatial AI, computer vision, or humanitarian tech. Past AI Club members used this experience as a foundation for spatial data projects that won regional science fairs.

featured.name: Hugging Face 'AI for Good' Hub
featured.desc: A curated collection of open-source models built for social good — including wildfire spread prediction, endangered species vocalization recognition, mental health screening tools, and medical imaging diagnostics. All models are forkable with documented training code.
featured.whyJoin: Fork a model that interests you, fine-tune it on local data, and deploy it as a Hugging Face Space. This gives you a live, shareable AI project in your portfolio within days — far more impressive than a GitHub repo alone. Recommended starting projects: wildlife detection (CS231N skill transfer) or text classification for social media misinformation.

localAction.intro: The best AI projects solve problems people actually have. Here's a playbook for approaching local organizations with AI solutions — with outreach templates and suggested project ideas for each.

orgs.name: Public Library
orgs.why: Libraries are trusted community institutions actively seeking to expand digital literacy programs. They have space, foot traffic, and existing relationships with underserved communities — the perfect environment to deploy and test AI tools.
suggestedProjects: AI-powered book recommendation system trained on the local catalog
Multilingual document translation kiosk for immigrant patrons
Digital literacy workshop: 'How AI Works' for seniors and families
orgs.outreachTemplate: Hi [Name], my name is [Your Name] from University High School's AI Club. We've built Uni AI Hub (https://uni-ai-hub-new.vercel.app) — a platform aggregating AI resources for students. We're now looking to apply what we've learned to benefit our community, and we'd love to partner with [Library Name] on a [specific project idea]. Could we schedule a 15-minute call to explore this? I can come by the library in person if that's more convenient.
tips: Start with a digital literacy workshop — it's low-risk for them and high-value for building trust
Bring a working demo, even a prototype, to the first meeting
Ask about their biggest operational pain points before pitching — listen first

orgs.name: Braille Institute / Blind Association
orgs.why: AI has transformative potential for accessibility — from real-time audio descriptions to document reading and navigation assistance. Organizations serving the visually impaired are often eager to test new assistive technologies but lack technical capacity to develop them.
suggestedProjects: AI image captioning tool that describes photos and documents aloud
Audio-first interface for navigating local government websites
Object detection app for indoor navigation assistance
orgs.outreachTemplate: Hi [Name], I'm a high school student from University High School's AI Club, and I'm reaching out because I'm deeply interested in using AI for accessibility. Our club has been studying computer vision and natural language processing, and I think there's an opportunity to build a tool that could meaningfully help your clients. Specifically, I'm thinking about [specific project idea — e.g., an image description tool]. I'd love to get your perspective on what would be most useful. Would you be open to a brief conversation?
tips: Reach out to the Braille Institute of America — they have chapters in LA/Orange County
Involve potential users in the design process from day one — don't build in isolation
Focus on one specific, well-scoped problem rather than a broad 'accessibility platform'

orgs.name: Animal Shelter
orgs.why: Animal shelters face real operational challenges — predicting adoption likelihood, managing volunteer schedules, identifying animals from blurry intake photos. Many are underfunded and understaffed, making them open to free technical help with measurable outcomes.
suggestedProjects: Animal-adopter matching algorithm based on behavioral data and adopter preferences
Computer vision tool for automatic animal photo tagging and profile generation
Predictive model for adoption likelihood to prioritize outreach efforts
orgs.outreachTemplate: Hi [Name], I'm [Your Name], a student at University High School where I lead our AI Club. We're working on a project to help local nonprofits use AI to improve their operations, and [Shelter Name] came up as an organization doing incredible work. I'd love to explore whether we could build something useful for you — for example, a tool to help match animals with the right adopters, or to automate some of the photo tagging for your online listings. It would be a free student project. Would you be open to a 20-minute conversation?
tips: Contact the volunteer coordinator, not the executive director, for your first outreach
Ask to shadow staff for a few hours to understand their real workflow before proposing a solution
Orange County Animal Care and LA Animal Services are both large organizations actively working on digital improvements

orgs.name: Senior Center
orgs.why: Senior centers serve one of the communities most impacted by — and most wary of — AI. Teaching seniors to use AI tools safely builds digital confidence and reduces isolation. You'll also gain perspective on how to design AI for non-technical users.
suggestedProjects: Monthly 'AI Essentials' workshop series tailored for seniors (ChatGPT, scam detection, health info)
Voice-first AI assistant for medication reminders and appointment scheduling
Intergenerational storytelling project: AI transcription and preservation of oral histories
orgs.outreachTemplate: Hi [Name], I'm a student from University High School's AI Club. We're looking for opportunities to give back to the community by sharing what we know about artificial intelligence. I'd love to offer a free, friendly workshop for your members — something like 'What is AI and how can it help me?' — completely non-technical, focused on practical everyday uses. We'd tailor it entirely to what your community needs. Would this be something [Center Name] might be interested in?
tips: Don't use jargon — ever. Practice explaining your topic to a grandparent before the workshop
Bring printed handouts; don't assume everyone has a smartphone or laptop
This experience looks excellent in college applications as evidence of community engagement and communication skills
